Name: compressibility_via_entropy_allocation
Title: Optimizing Model Compression Through Entropy-Based Bit Allocation
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging entropy measures as a basis for bit allocation in model compression, we can achieve more efficient and interpretable compression schemes that respect the rate-distortion trade-offs inherent to the task at hand.
Related Work: Existing literature primarily focuses on mixed precision quantization and heuristic methods for model compression, such as SNIP and AMP-ViT. However, these works do not fully explore the theoretical bounds of compression schemes informed by entropy measures. This proposal distinguishes itself by framing bit allocation decisions through a rigorous information-theoretic lens, providing a foundation for understanding the trade-offs involved in model compression.
Abstract: This research proposes a novel approach to model compression that emphasizes the role of entropy in guiding bit allocation for different components of a model. Traditional compression methods often rely on empirical heuristics, which can lead to suboptimal performance and lack of interpretability. By framing compression as a rate-distortion problem, we derive an entropy-based framework for allocating bits to model parameters, ensuring that more critical components receive sufficient bits to maintain predictive performance. We will explore the implications of this framework through a series of experiments that evaluate the trade-offs between model size and accuracy, quantifying the achievable compression rates against theoretical limits. Our findings aim to provide a deeper understanding of the information-theoretic capacities of various compression techniques and offer practical guidelines for achieving efficient model compression that is grounded in a solid mathematical foundation.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline model compression technique (e.g., traditional pruning) for comparison.
2. Develop an entropy-based bit allocation strategy, calculating the entropy of model parameters relative to their contribution to predictive accuracy.
3. Apply the proposed method on benchmark datasets and models, measuring performance using accuracy, model size, and compression rate metrics.
4. Compare the results against traditional methods to validate the efficiency and effectiveness of the entropy-based approach.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the difficulty of accurately estimating entropy in high-dimensional parameter spaces, which could lead to suboptimal bit allocation. Additionally, the approach may not generalize well to all model architectures, particularly those with complex interactions between parameters. Limitations may also arise from the need for a well-defined distortion measure, which may not always be easily obtainable.
